q: A camera is an optical instrument that forms and records an image of an object.
The image recorded by a camera may only be stored digitally, not on film.
All cameras form images in fundamentally different ways depending on whether film or an electronic sensor is used.
A convex lens in a camera forms a real, inverted image on the film or electronic sensor.
The diaphragm in a camera is placed in front of the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
The aperture of a camera is a rectangular opening through which light passes.
The aperture can be made wide or narrow by adjusting the shutter.
The f-number of a camera is the ratio of the diameter of the aperture to the focal length of the lens.
A smaller f-number indicates a narrower aperture.
When taking a photograph in bright light, a camera should use a small f-number (wide aperture).
When taking a photograph in dim light, a camera should use a large f-number (narrow aperture).
The shutter controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
Shutter speed is expressed in terms of the aperture size.
A shorter exposure time corresponds to a lower shutter speed.
The focusing ring adjusts the distance between the lens and the film to achieve a sharp image.
The film at the back of the camera functions as a screen that reflects the image.
Camera film consists of a thin layer of chemical compounds of gold.
Image formation on film occurs due to a physical change in the silver compounds.
In a digital camera, an electronic sensor replaces the film.
Electronic sensors in digital cameras are made up of millions of large pixels which do not respond to light.
For a distant object, its image in a camera is formed at the focus of the lens.
The f-number specifies the shutter speed of a camera.
